observer_reasoning: Under the doctrine of equitable conversion (the traditional common-law rule tested on many property exams), once a valid land sale contract is signed, the buyer obtains equitable title and bears the risk of loss, while the seller retains legal title as security until closing. Therefore, if the barn burned after the contract was formed but before closing, the buyer bears the loss if he held equitable title at that time. In that case he must still complete the purchase and cannot recover the down payment. Choice C matches this rule. The model’s reasoning accurately explains equitable conversion and correctly applies it to the facts, leading to the correct answer. Thus the probability the answer is correct is very high. The model’s tone is confident and declarative without hedging, indicating a high self-reported confidence level.
subject_reasoning: The buyer will be unsuccessful in his suit for a refund of his down payment if he held equitable title to the farm on January 20, as the risk of loss would have passed to him. Equitable title typically passes to the buyer upon the signing of an enforceable contract, which in this case would have been on January 1. Therefore, on January 20, the buyer held equitable title, and the loss due to the fire was his to bear, obliging him to complete the contract.
